name: Mexico
tactical_document:
# Mexico — Tactical Profile

## Identity & Philosophy
Hosts of the 2026 World Cup and the most-supported team in CONCACAF. Returning veteran **Javier Aguirre** ("El Vasco") was brought back to steady a team that had lost its identity after disastrous Copa Américas — and he has delivered. Aguirre's Mexico is **possession-based but pragmatic**: technically excellent in midfield, comfortable building from the back, but with the defensive discipline and tactical fouling of a manager who learned in Spain. The team plays a narrow 4-3-3 that funnels the ball through the half-spaces, leans on a physical holding midfielder as the defensive anchor, and looks to release the wide forwards on the counter. As hosts in Mexico City, Mexico carry the loudest crowd in the tournament and the highest baseline expectation — a deep run is demanded.

## Round of 16 Lineup & Context (vs England, July 5 — Estadio Azteca, Mexico City)
Mexico reached the Round of 16 as **Group A winners with a perfect record** — nine points, three wins, three clean sheets — then won their Round-of-32 tie to set up a **win-or-go-home** heavyweight clash with England in front of a frenzied home crowd at the Azteca. Mexico carry a **clean bill of health** into the tie; the only selection debate is at the base of midfield / centre-back. Aguirre keeps faith with the settled block:
- **Raúl Rangel** keeps goal — he has yet to concede in his debut World Cup campaign; 40-year-old **Guillermo Ochoa** (a record sixth World Cup) is the experienced backup.
- The first-choice centre-back pairing of **César Montes** and **Johan Vásquez** is retained — Montes edges out Edson Álvarez for the starting CB slot, and against an England side that floods crosses into the box, Montes's aerial dominance is essential.
- **Edson Álvarez** does NOT start: he recovered from February ankle surgery but lacks match sharpness, so Aguirre trusts **Erik Lira** as the holding-midfield anchor screening the back four.
- **Luis Romo** takes the box-to-box role alongside Lira and the creator Mora — powerful, two-way, comfortable dropping to cover.
- **Jorge Sánchez** starts at right back over Israel Reyes; **Jesús Gallardo** continues at left back.
- **Raúl Jiménez** leads the line — his Premier League experience against familiar English foes and his hold-up play anchor the front three; he is the primary penalty taker.
- **Julián Quiñones** continues as the in-form goal threat cutting in from the left; **Roberto Alvarado** is the wide spark on the right.

## Formation
- Shape: **4-3-3** with a narrow midfield (the three CMs are tight); compresses to **4-1-4-1** out of possession with Lira shielding.
- Role mapping (roster order in `mexico.yaml`):
  - index 0: GK — **Raúl Rangel** (#1) — first-choice shot-stopper; yet to concede at this World Cup; good with feet for short build-up, not a sweeper.
  - index 1: LB — **Jesús Gallardo** (#23) — experienced overlapping fullback; provides natural width on the left because the LW cuts inside.
  - index 2: LCB — **Johan Vásquez** (#5) — ball-playing CB, comfortable carrying out, the primary progressor from the back.
  - index 3: RCB — **César Montes** (#3) — first-choice centre-back; dominant aerial defender (strength 17), the no-nonsense stopper alongside Vásquez's progressor; edges out Álvarez to start.
  - index 4: RB — **Jorge Sánchez** (#19) — disciplined two-way right back; positionally sound first, picks his moments to overlap behind the RW.
  - index 5: DM/6 — **Erik Lira** (#6) — the **holding-midfield anchor** standing in for Álvarez; disciplined ball-winner, screens the back four, steps out to break up play, recycles short.
  - index 6: CM/8/10 — **Gilberto Mora** (#16) — the young #10 operating from the left interior, the team's chief creator from midfield; gets between the lines, slips passes into the forwards.
  - index 7: CM/8 — **Luis Romo** (#18) — the right-sided box-to-box #8; powerful, two-footed, two-way engine (strength 15, stamina 16); circulates possession, arrives late, and can drop to cover for Lira.
  - index 8: LW — **Julián Quiñones** (#22) — mobile, in-form goalscorer; cuts inside off the left, runs in behind, and can rotate with Jiménez.
  - index 9: CF — **Raúl Jiménez** (#9) — physical, hold-up #9, target man for crosses and second balls; the veteran centre-forward and primary penalty taker.
  - index 10: RW — **Roberto Alvarado** (#25) — "El Piojo"; tricky, two-footed dribbler (dribbling 16), direct off the right, the wide spark on the counter and a key playmaker.

## Style of Play

### Build-up
**Patient short build-up.** Rangel splits the CBs wide, Lira drops between them to form a 3+1, Gallardo and Sánchez push high to give width. **Vásquez is the primary progressor** — he carries forward into midfield with the ball at his feet (Montes stays as the deeper, safer anchor of the pair). Mexico is comfortable in long possession sequences (15+ passes), waiting for the opposition press to fatigue before going vertical. When pressed hard, the first option is back to Rangel, then a switch to the opposite fullback.

### Pressing
**Mid-block first, selective high press.** Trigger: opposition CB taking a heavy first touch — Jiménez and Quiñones squeeze, Mora jumps into midfield. The press is **possession-oriented** rather than chaos-oriented — Mexico wants to win the ball back to play, not just disrupt. Lira does not press; he sits in front of the back four as the safety net.

### Defensive shape
Compact **4-1-4-1** with Lira as the single pivot. The other two CMs (Mora, Romo) drop to form a flat midfield four with Quiñones and Alvarado (or Jiménez dropping). The block is **mid-to-low**, conservative — Aguirre is happy to absorb pressure and counter through Quiñones and Alvarado. Mexico's defensive record is built on second-half control. **Tactical fouling** is encouraged: a yellow card to break a counter-attack on Lira or Romo is a feature, not a failure.

### Wide play
Asymmetric. **Left** = Quiñones inside + Gallardo overlap; this is where Mexico's chance creation happens via cut-backs from the byline. **Right** = Sánchez overlap + Alvarado wide-and-inside hybrid. Mora drifts to the left half-space to combine with Quiñones-Gallardo.

### Final third
Patterns: Quiñones cut-back from the left byline to Mora arriving at the top of the box; Mora through-ball to Jiménez peeling off the CB's shoulder; Alvarado running the channel between fullback and centre-back onto a Vásquez diagonal; switch of play from Lira to Sánchez isolating the opposition LB. Mexico will also drop second balls into Jiménez and play off his knock-downs.

## Set Pieces
- **Set-piece danger team.** Aguirre's Mexico is among the best in CONCACAF on dead balls — likely a key route to goal in a tight knockout.
- Attacking corners: **Mora** delivers from the right (in-swinger), **Romo** from the left (in-swinger). Primary aerial targets: Montes (front post), Jiménez (penalty spot), Vásquez (back post), Romo (second-phase edge of box).
- Defending corners: **man-marking** with two zonal at the front post. Montes takes the most dangerous opposing aerial target; Lira patrols the edge of the box.
- Free kicks: **Mora** takes direct from any zone within 28 units; **Romo** delivers wide free kicks.
- Penalties: **Jiménez** primary, **Quiñones** secondary, **Mora** tertiary.

## decide() Decision Priorities
Concrete rules the LLM should encode:
1. **If my player_id ends with "_5" (DM, jersey #6 — Lira) and the opposition has the ball within 10 units of the centre circle:** Move to intercept the passing lane to the opposition #10. Tackle if within 5 units.
2. **If my player_id ends with "_8" (LW, jersey #22 — Quiñones) and team_phase == "transition_attack":** Sprint forward on the diagonal cutting inside — the through-ball or shot is the priority over wide play.
3. **If my player_id ends with "_2" (LCB, jersey #5 — Vásquez) and team_phase == "building_up" and no opponent within 8 units:** Carry the ball forward into midfield. Do not pass short. (Montes "_3" stays back as cover — he does NOT carry; he passes short or clears.)
4. **If my role is GK (player_id ends with "_0" — Rangel) and team_phase == "building_up":** Pass short to the nearest unmarked CB. Avoid long balls unless press intensity is high.
5. **If my player_id ends with "_6" (CM, jersey #16 — Mora) and I receive the ball between the lines:** Prefer a forward through-ball Pass to a forward running in behind (CF "_9" Jiménez or LW "_8" Quiñones) over a recycle.
6. **If team_phase == "defending" and the opposition counter-attacks:** Nearest midfielder commits a tactical foul (Tackle) on the ball-carrier within 5 units of the centre circle.
7. **If my player_id ends with "_9" (CF, jersey #9 — Jiménez) and a teammate delivers a cross:** attack the penalty spot or back post — not the near post.
8. **If my player_id ends with "_1" (LB, jersey #23 — Gallardo) and team_phase == "attacking" and the LW (player_id ends with "_8") has the ball inside:** Sprint forward and outside the LW (overlap to byline).
9. **If my role is MID and team has held possession > 30 seconds:** keep circulating the ball — patience over verticality unless a clear through-ball is available.
10. **If team is leading by 1+ goals and minute > 75:** Compress the block. The DM (player_id ends with "_5" — Lira) drops between the CBs to form a 5-3-1 if needed. Tactical fouling intensifies. (Mexico are elite at shutting down second halves — protect the lead.)
11. **If my player_id ends with "_10" (RW, jersey #25 — Alvarado) and the ball is on the left:** make a blindside run between LB and LCB.
12. **Set pieces / penalties / direct free kicks within 28 units:** defer to the CM (player_id ends with "_6" — Mora) for creation and the CF (player_id ends with "_9" — Jiménez) for penalties.

## Key Player Notes
- **Erik Lira (index 5, #6):** The holding-midfield anchor standing in for the unfit Edson Álvarez. Disciplined ball-winner (discipline 15, stamina 16); screens the back four, recycles short, and steps out to break up play. The single pivot in the 4-1-4-1 — drops between the CBs to protect a lead.
- **César Montes (index 3, #3):** First-choice centre-back; edges out Álvarez to start. The dominant aerial stopper (strength 17); pairs with Vásquez (the progressor). Stays deep — does not carry.
- **Quiñones (index 8, #22):** In-form goalscorer; the team's primary counter-attack outlet from the left, rotates with Jiménez, secondary penalty taker.
- **Mora (index 6, #16):** The teenage creator — the youngest player at the tournament. The team's chief creator from open play and primary set-piece deliverer.
- **Luis Romo (index 7, #18):** The two-way engine. Powerful box-to-box #8 (strength 15, stamina 16); recycles possession, arrives late in the box, and drops to cover for Lira when Mexico defend.
- **Vásquez (index 2, #5):** The ball-playing CB. Licensed to carry into midfield.
- **Jiménez (index 9, #9):** Target man and primary penalty taker; leads the line against familiar English opposition. Wins the aerial duels that set up second-phase attacks.
- **Alvarado (index 10, #25):** The wide spark on the right. Dribbling 16, two-footed; isolates the opposition LB, provides Sánchez defensive help, and feeds cut-backs — his playmaking quality is crucial.

## Tournament Mindset
Mexico carry the largest crowd in the tournament at the Azteca and the most fervent expectation in CONCACAF. **Group A winners with a perfect record and three clean sheets**, they enter the Round of 16 against England full of belief — but it is single-elimination, and the home-crowd pressure cuts both ways. Aguirre will play pragmatically: controlled possession, mid-block defending, ruthless second-half game management, tactical fouling, and one or two moments of brilliance from Mora or Quiñones. Set-piece goals and clean-sheet discipline are the blueprint. England will float crosses at Montes; Mexico must win those duels, ride the Azteca, and advance.
